title-group.article-title: Prevalence and impact of long COVID on health-related quality of life in previously hospitalized COVID-19 patients: a 2-year follow-up study

contrib-group.aff: 1https://ror.org/01znkr924grid.10223.320000 0004 1937 0490Division of Respiratory Disease and Tuberculosis, Department of Medicine, Faculty of Medicine Siriraj Hospital, Mahidol University, Bangkok, Thailand
2https://ror.org/01znkr924grid.10223.320000 0004 1937 0490Division of Ambulatory Medicine, Department of Medicine, Faculty of Medicine Siriraj Hospital, Mahidol University, Bangkok, Thailand
3https://ror.org/03e2qe334grid.412029.c0000 0000 9211 2704Present Address: Department of Medicine, Faculty of Medicine, Naresuan University, Phitsanulok, Thailand

license.license-p: Open Access This article is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-NoDerivatives 4.0 International License, which permits any non-commercial use, sharing, distribution and reproduction in any medium or format, as long as you give appropriate credit to the original author(s) and the source, provide a link to the Creative Commons licence, and indicate if you modified the licensed material. You do not have permission under this licence to share adapted material derived from this article or parts of it. The images or other third party material in this article are included in the article’s Creative Commons licence, unless indicated otherwise in a credit line to the material. If material is not included in the article’s Creative Commons licence and your intended use is not permitted by statutory regulation or exceeds the permitted use, you will need to obtain permission directly from the copyright holder. To view a copy of this licence, visit http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/.

abstract.p: Long COVID is a major health concern, yet evidence from Southeast Asia remains limited. We evaluated the prevalence, predictors, and health-related quality of life (HRQoL) impact of long COVID among hospitalized adults in Thailand over two years. This single-center, ambidirectional cohort combined retrospective chart review with prospective follow-up. The cohort comprised 295 adults hospitalized for symptomatic COVID-19 between August and November 2021. Interviews occurred at three months, one year, and two years post-infection. Long-COVID prevalence was 49.8% at three months; among these, 64.4% reported persistent symptoms at one year, and 22% of the one-year symptomatic group had symptoms at two years. Common symptoms included breathlessness, fatigue, and memory disturbance. Logistic regression identified severe–critical acute illness as a risk factor (adjusted odds ratio [aOR] 2.06, 95% CI 1.23–3.46), while full vaccination was protective (aOR 0.48, 95% CI 0.25–0.90). Long COVID was associated with lower HRQoL across all EQ-5D-5L domains. Long COVID was frequent among hospitalized Thai patients, with symptoms persisting in a substantial proportion up to two years. Severity of acute COVID-19 and vaccination status predicted long COVID. Long COVID was linked to reduced HRQoL, underscoring the need for follow-up and further confirmation in larger, multicenter studies.

sec.title: Supplementary Information
sec.p: The online version contains supplementary material available at 10.1038/s41598-025-31598-7.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: The pandemic of severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV-2) has posed a significant challenge to humanity since 2019. The cumulative number of Coronavirus Disease 2019 (COVID-19) cases worldwide has passed 700 million in early 20241. Despite a decrease in mortality, long COVID has been a perpetuating threat to the survivors. Reported prevalence of long COVID varies because a consensus definition was lacking early in the outbreak. Earlier studies often defined long COVID (also known as post-COVID-19 condition) as the persistence of at least one symptom for 28 days after the onset of acute COVID-19. Later consensus definition describes long COVID as new or persistent symptoms occurring beyond three months after infection2. Using a mix of definitions, the pooled prevalence estimate was 43%3, with higher prevalence in hospitalized COVID-19 cases (54% versus 34%). Studies in European countries demonstrated that the presence of long COVID has a significant impact on health-related quality of life (HRQoL)4,5.

Several studies have been conducted to identify risk factors for long COVID. Despite high study heterogeneity, female sex and early COVID-19 severity have been reported as predictors for long COVID6,7. Protective effect of COVID-19 vaccination against long COVID has also been reported8. Patients who received COVID-19 vaccination before infection had a lower incidence of long COVID.

Studies on the prevalence, predictors, and impact of long COVID in low- and middle-income countries are limited, especially in the Southeast Asian region. A multicenter study in India reported a prevalence of long COVID of 16.5% one year after hospital discharge for acute COVID-19. Intensive care unit admission and prolonged hospitalization were associated with the persistence of symptoms at the one-year follow-up9. In the African region, a systematic review estimated the incidence of long COVID symptoms at 48.6%, based on studies with a median follow-up time of three months. The meta-regression analysis further demonstrated significant associations between older age, hospitalization during acute infection, and the frequency of long COVID10. The reported prevalence of long COVID in Southeast Asia ranges from 3.4% to 47.0%11–16. However, the studies are highly heterogeneous and lack long-term follow-up. Moreover, evidence regarding the impact of long COVID on HRQoL remains limited.

This current study was conducted in Thailand with the aims to determine the prevalence of long COVID in hospitalized COVID-19 patients, identify the predictors for developing long COVID and elucidate the impact on HRQoL during the follow-up period of 2 years.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: From 572 patients with acute COVID-19 infections, a total of 295 participants were recruited in the study. Long COVID symptoms were reported by 147 participants (49.8%) three months post-infection. Of the participants exhibiting long COVID symptoms at three months, 59.2% (87 out of 147) responded to the follow-up interview, and 64.4% (56 out of 87) reported persistent symptoms one year later. Of the participants reporting persistent long COVID one year post-infection, 73.2% (41 out of 56) responded to the two-year post-infection interview. Among the responders at the two-year follow-up, 22% (9 out of 41) reported persistent symptoms (Fig. 1).

More than half of the participants were classified as severe to critical acute COVID-19 with 23.4% rate of high-flow oxygen via nasal cannula prescription and 5.1% rate of invasive mechanical ventilation. The most frequent vaccination status was no vaccination at any dose, accounting for 52.5% of participants. Among the vaccinated, 29.5% received one dose, 17.3% received two doses, and 0.7% received three doses (Table 1). All vaccines included in this study are those administered in a two-dose regimen, consistent with recommended primary series schedules. The most common vaccine type was the adenovirus-vectored vaccine, followed by the inactivated virus vaccine. Among participants who received one dose, 94.3% received the adenovirus-vectored vaccine. Of those who completed two doses, 60.8% received two doses of the adenovirus-vectored vaccine, while 33.3% received two doses of the inactivated virus vaccine. The remainder received mixed vaccine regimens. Baseline and hospitalization characteristics between responders and non-responders at the one-year and two-year follow-ups were compared (Table S1 and S2). At one year post-infection, age, comorbidities, and receipt of antiviral therapy during hospitalization differed significantly between responders and non-responders. In contrast, no significant differences were observed at two years post-infection.

caption.p: Baseline and hospitalization characteristics of the participants.

sec.p: At three months post-infection, the estimated prevalence of long COVID in hospitalized COVID-19 cases was 49.8% (95% CI, 44.0–55.7). The five most common long COVID symptoms included breathlessness (30.5%), hair loss (18.6%), fatigue (14.6%), muscle pain (11.9%), and chest pain (6.8%) (Table 2). Among participants who exhibited long COVID symptoms three months post-infection, the crude and inverse probability weighting (IPW)-weighted prevalence estimates of persistent long COVID one year post-infection were 64.4% (95% CI, 53.3–74.1) and 65.0% (95% CI, 54.4–75.6), respectively (Table S3). It was worth noting that neurocognitive symptoms became more common one year post-infection. The five most commonly reported persistent long COVID symptoms one year post-infection included breathlessness (47.1%), memory disturbance (28.7%), fatigue (24.1%), cough (19.5%), and peripheral numbness (19.5%). Among participants who reported persistent symptoms one year post-infection, the crude and IPW-weighted prevalence estimates of persistent long COVID at two years post-infection were 22.0% (95% CI, 11.1–38.0) and 20.9% (95% CI, 8.5–33.4), respectively (Table S4).

caption.p: Estimated prevalence of long COVID symptoms in hospitalized acute COVID-19 patients three months after the onset of acute COVID-19.

sec.p: Of the 147 participants reporting long COVID symptoms, 46.9% (69 participants) indicated that these symptoms significantly impacted their daily living. Fatigue, sleep disturbance, decrease in appetite, joint pain, and muscle pain were reported significantly more frequently in those reporting impact on daily living compared with those reporting no impact (Table 3). After Benjamini–Hochberg adjustment for multiple testing, only joint pain remained significantly associated with perceived impact on daily living. Although breathlessness was the most commonly reported symptom, there was no statistically significant difference between participants with long COVID who reported an impact on daily living and those who did not.

caption.p: Proportions of long COVID symptoms among participants who reported symptoms with and without a perceived impact on daily living.

table-wrap-foot.p: *Fisher’s exact test: p-value < 0.05; Benjamini–Hochberg adjusted p-value < 0.05.

sec.p: Multiple logistic regression was employed to identify predictive factors for long COVID and its impact on daily living. The independent variables included age, sex, body mass index (BMI), Charlson Comorbidity Score, severity of acute COVID-19, and vaccination status. Severity of acute COVID-19 was categorized as binary: non-severe versus severe to critical, according to World Health Organization (WHO) criteria. Complete vaccination was defined as receiving at least two doses of the vaccines before the onset of COVID-19. Three participants with missing BMI data were excluded from the analysis.

A total of 292 participants were included in the analysis to identify predictive factors, with 50.3% reporting long COVID symptoms and 23.6% indicating that these symptoms affected their daily living. The mean age of participants was 56.9 years (SD = 18.4 years), with 40.4% male, 57.9% having experienced severe to critical COVID-19, and 30.9% fully vaccinated. The mean BMI and Charlson Comorbidity Score were 26.2 kg/m² (SD = 6.1 kg/m2) and 2.7 (SD = 2.1), respectively.

Both severe to critical COVID-19 and complete vaccination status were significant predictors of long COVID (Table 4). Participants with severe to critical COVID-19 had a higher risk of developing long COVID symptoms (adjusted odds ratio [aOR] 2.06, 95% CI 1.23–3.46; p = 0.006). Conversely, complete vaccination provided protection against long COVID (aOR 0.48, 95% CI 0.25–0.90; p = 0.024). No multicollinearity was detected, and none of the tested interaction terms were statistically significant. Model fit was evaluated using both the deviance goodness-of-fit test and the Hosmer–Lemeshow test. The deviance test was statistically significant (p = 0.011), suggesting some lack of fit, whereas the Hosmer–Lemeshow test (p = 0.939) indicated good fit. Considering the large sample size and the greater robustness of the Hosmer–Lemeshow test in logistic regression, the overall model fit was deemed adequate.

caption.p: Predictors of long COVID: multivariable analysis of 292 hospitalized COVID-19 patients.

tr.td: Complete vaccination; 2 or more doses (ref: no vaccination or 1 dose)

td.bold: Predictors of long COVID with reported impact on daily living

tr.td: Complete vaccination; 2 or more doses (ref: no vaccination or 1 dose)

sec.p: Similar predictive results were observed for long COVID with reported impact on daily living: severe to critical COVID-19 was significantly predictive of long COVID affecting daily living (aOR 2.02, 95% CI 1.08–3.86; p = 0.030), while complete vaccination significantly reduced the risk (aOR 0.29, 95% CI 0.10–0.72; p = 0.015). Additionally, male sex was associated with a lower risk of long COVID with impact on daily living (aOR 0.55, 95% CI 0.30–0.98; p = 0.048) (Table 4).

The impact of long COVID symptoms on HRQoL was measured using the EuroQol-5 Dimensions-5 Levels (EQ-5D-5L) questionnaire. Our findings indicated that long COVID symptoms adversely affect HRQoL (Fig. 2and Table S5). Notably, similar results were observed at both one and two years post-infection (Figure S1 and S2). At three months post-infection, participants with long COVID were significantly more likely to report problems in the mobility, self-care, usual activities, and pain/discomfort domains of the EQ-5D-5L compared with those without long COVID. At one year post-infection (restricted to participants with long COVID at three months), significant differences were observed in the usual activities and anxiety/depression domains. At two years post-infection (restricted to participants with long COVID at one year), problems in usual activities and pain/discomfort remained significantly more common among those with long COVID (Table 5).

caption.p: EQ-5D-5L responses in participants with and without long COVID symptoms three months post-infection.

td.bold: One year post-infection (among participants with long COVID at three months)
Two years post-infection among participants with long COVID at one year

table-wrap-foot.p: *Fisher’s exact test p-value < 0.05; Benjamini–Hochberg adjusted p-value < 0.05.

sec.p: The presence of long COVID symptoms significantly reduced utility scores at all time points: three months, one year, and two years post-infection (Fig. 3). This trend continued with persistent long COVID symptoms measured at one and two years post-infection. The median EQ-5D-5L utility score among participants with long COVID at three months post-infection was 1.00 (IQR 0.93–1.00), compared with 1.00 (IQR 1.00–1.00) among those without long COVID (p < 0.001; Hodges–Lehmann difference < 0.001, 95% CI < 0.001 to < 0.001). At one year post-infection, the median utility score was 0.93 (IQR 0.79–1.00) in participants with long COVID and 1.00 (IQR 0.94–1.00) in those without (p = 0.001; Hodges–Lehmann difference = 0.06, 95% CI < 0.001 to 0.10). At two years post-infection, participants with long COVID had a median utility score of 0.86 (IQR 0.62–0.93), compared with 1.00 (IQR 1.00–1.00) among those without long COVID (p = 0.001; Hodges–Lehmann difference = 0.14, 95% CI 0.07 to 0.24). The results from the visual analog scale (VAS) corresponded well with the calculated utility scores. Utility trajectories showed that participants with persistent long COVID symptoms at 1 year had a worsening trend compared with those without symptoms. By 2 years, participants who no longer had symptoms showed improved utility scores, while those with persistent symptoms continued to have consistently lower values (Fig. 4).

caption.p: Utility outcomes among participants with and without long COVID symptoms. Panels (A–C) show the distribution of EQ-5D-5L utility scores at (A) 3 months, (B) 1 year, and (C) 2 years after infection. Panels (D–F) show the corresponding EQ-VAS scores for self-rated overall health at (D) 3 months, (E) 1 year, and (F) 2 years after infection. Box plots display the median and interquartile range. Panel (G) summarises mean utility scores across all time points and symptom groups.
Utility score trajectories between timepoints, coloured by symptom status at follow-up; medians with interquartile ranges are shown as thick lines.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: This study provides valuable insights into the long-term effects of COVID-19, specifically focusing on long COVID among hospitalized patients over a two-year follow-up period. Furthermore, it emphasizes the impact on daily living and HRQoL, which is a critical topic with limited data in low- and middle-income countries. Our research distinguishes itself from other studies in Thailand by concentrating exclusively on hospitalized COVID-19 patients.

Our findings indicate a long COVID prevalence of 49.8% at three months post-infection, which is consistent with previous research. A meta-analysis reporting a mean follow-up time of 126 days found a prevalence of 52.6%. Notably, the symptom distribution aligns with prior studies; breathlessness, fatigue, and memory disturbances were commonly reported by patients exhibiting long COVID symptoms17.

The literature presents a varied range of long COVID prevalence in Thailand and Southeast Asia, with figures ranging from 3.4% to 47.0%11–16. This variability may be attributed to differences in participant recruitment methodologies, as studies utilizing online surveys often report lower prevalence proportions11–13. Although different SARS-CoV-2 variants may influence these prevalence proportions of long COVID, a preceding study conducted during the Omicron wave reported a 47% prevalence of long COVID, comparable to our findings during the Delta variant surge15. Importantly, our study’s focus on hospitalized patients offers a more precise perspective on long COVID prevalence, revealing a prevalence of 38.2% in a mixed population from previous research which was also conducted during the Delta variant wave14.

Among participants with long COVID, those who indicated that their symptoms impacted their daily living frequently cited symptoms such as fatigue, sleep disturbances, decreased appetite, joint pain, and muscle pain. After adjustment for multiple testing, only joint pain remained significantly associated with perceived impact on daily living. Interestingly, there was no statistically significant difference in breathlessness prevalence between those reporting an impact on daily living and those who did not. This may reflect the absence of severity stratification, as the study did not assess the severity of breathlessness, which could explain the difference in perceived impact.

This study corroborates previous findings regarding predictors of long COVID, identifying severe to critical COVID-19 as a significant risk factor and highlighting vaccination as a protective element18–23. Our analysis did not find age, sex, BMI, or Charlson Comorbidity Score to be predictive for long COVID overall. However, when considering participants who reported an impact on daily living, female sex emerged as another significant predictor. Potential explanations for this discrepancy may include differences in inflammatory profiles and airway morphology between genders, which could contribute to the increased risk of long COVID in women24,25.

Over the two-year follow-up, 64.4% of participants with long COVID reported persistent symptoms one year post-infection, of whom 22.0% continued to report symptoms two years post-infection. These results reinforce concerns that long COVID symptoms can endure for two to three years following initial infection26,27. Our study demonstrates significant adverse effects of long COVID on HRQoL, with utility scores reflecting lower HRQoL across all domains evaluated by the EQ-5D-5L, consistent with findings from other studies focusing on hospitalized COVID-19 patients from other countries4,5. Differences in EQ-5D-5L utility scores between participants with and without long COVID were statistically significant across all follow-up points. At three months, the estimated Hodges–Lehmann location shift was negligible and unlikely to be clinically relevant. At one year, the estimated group difference was 0.06, and at two years the difference was 0.15, both exceeding the classic minimally important difference for EQ-5D utilities of 0.03–0.0728,29, indicating that these differences are clinically meaningful.

Our study has several strengths. Firstly, the two-year prospective follow-up of long COVID patients fills a data gap in low- and middle-income countries, highlighting the reality of persistent long COVID symptoms. Secondly, we specifically addressed the impact of long COVID on daily living, revealing that nearly half of the participants considered their condition a significant issue. Additionally, the use of a validated questionnaire to assess HRQoL impacts enhances the robustness of our findings and provides a framework for future studies on the societal and economic implications of long COVID. Lastly, focusing solely on hospitalized patients enhances the relevance of our findings to similar populations.

Certain limitations must be acknowledged. The small sample size restricted the power to detect differences between groups, particularly in the context of multiple testing and further analyses such as regression models to examine associations between patient characteristics and declines in utility scores. The absence of data from individuals who were unable to communicate or who declined participation at 3 months limits our ability to fully assess potential selection bias. In addition, the high loss-to-follow-up rate limits the accuracy of prevalence estimates. Nevertheless, the IPW-weighted estimates were close to the crude estimates, which increases confidence in the reliability of our findings. Another limitation is the generalizability of the findings. The specific population studied limits the applicability of our findings to non-hospitalized individuals. Furthermore, the study was conducted during the Delta variant outbreak, and the prevalence and impact of long COVID may differ with other variants. Moreover, the high vaccination rates in the current context may affect the present prevalence of long COVID compared to our study. The protective effect of vaccination against long COVID symptoms may be underestimated due to the lack of data on the timing between vaccination and infection onset. Nonetheless, the definition of complete vaccination was based on the recommended two-dose regimen for each vaccine, supported by clinical efficacy data, which supports the validity of the analysis conclusions30. Because relevant potential factors (e.g., socioeconomic status, occupation, smoking history, reinfection, and mental health comorbidity) were unavailable for inclusion in the logistic regression analysis, the effects of other confounders cannot be excluded. While telephone interviews might introduce variability in data collection, the reliability of our trained interviewers helped mitigate this concern. Additionally, the lack of data on repeated COVID-19 infections leaves open the question of how reinfections might influence long-term symptoms. Lastly, we did not include a comparator group of people without COVID-19, thus we cannot affirm that the reported symptoms were solely the sequelae of COVID-19.

sec.title: Study Design, Participants, and data collection
sec.p: This single-center ambidirectional cohort study was approved by the institutional review board at the Faculty of Medicine Siriraj Hospital, Mahidol University (Certificate of Approval number: Si 961/2021). The study was conducted at Siriraj Hospital in Bangkok, Thailand. All data collection processes were carried out following ethical approval, with informed consent obtained verbally from participants. The participant recruitment period spanned from December 8, 2021, to February 22, 2022, with the last follow-up date occurring on January 3, 2024. Medical records were first accessed for research purposes on December 8, 2021. Individual participant identification data were retrieved for a retrospective chart review and telephone interviews. Prior to analysis, participant de-identification was performed to protect confidentiality. All research was performed in accordance with relevant guidelines and regulations, including the Declaration of Helsinki.

All consecutive patients with COVID-19 who were admitted to the medical wards and intensive care units of Siriraj Hospital from August 2021 to November 2021 and were discharged alive were eligible for the study. The inclusion criteria included individuals who were 18 years or older, those with a confirmed positive SARS-CoV-2 real-time Reverse Transcription Polymerase Chain Reaction (RT-PCR) result from a respiratory specimen, and those presenting with at least one COVID-19 symptom upon admission. Because healthcare resource allocation was complex during the COVID-19 outbreak, and many patients admitted to Siriraj Hospital were not covered under its reimbursement plan, follow-up was therefore conducted by telephone interview, with verbal consent obtained directly from patients during the first telephone contact and documented in the case record form. Interviews were not conducted with patients who declined to participate, and those who were unable to communicate were also excluded from the study.

A total of three telephone interviews were performed by a pulmonary fellow and a trained research assistant. The first interview occurred around three months after the onset of COVID-19. All participants who reported long COVID symptoms at three months underwent follow-up interviews one year post-infection. Subsequently, the participants with persistent long COVID symptoms at one year underwent another follow-up interview two years post-infection. Participants were asked about the symptoms of long COVID and their impact on daily living. HRQoL was assessed using the Thai-validated EQ-5D-5L questionnaire31. All demographic data, such as sex, age at infection, BMI, comorbidity, vaccination status before the onset of COVID-19, and clinical information during hospitalization including managements for COVID-19 were retrospectively reviewed from Siriraj Hospital medical records.

Severity of COVID-19 was defined according to the WHO’s living guidance for clinical management of COVID-1932. Severe COVID-19 was defined as the presence of clinical signs of pneumonia accompanied by at least one of the following criteria: a respiratory rate exceeding 30 breaths per minute, severe respiratory distress, or pulse oxygen saturation below 90% in ambient air. Critical COVID-19 encompassed patients exhibiting clinical signs of acute respiratory distress syndrome, sepsis, or septic shock. Cases of pneumonia without any indicators of severe COVID-19 were categorized as moderate COVID-19. Patients presenting without signs of pneumonia or hypoxemia were classified as having mild disease. In this study, mild and moderate diseases were grouped together and referred to as non-severe COVID-19.

sec.title: Study outcomes
sec.p: The primary outcome of this study was the prevalence of long COVID. The secondary outcomes included the impact of long COVID symptoms on daily living, HRQoL, predictive factors of long COVID, and the prevalence of persistent long COVID at one year and two years post-infection.

The definition of long COVID was adopted from the definition of post-COVID-19 syndrome proposed by the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence33. In this study, long COVID was defined as the presence of at least one symptom that develops during or after an onset of COVID-19 and continues for more than 12 weeks. The symptoms included cough, sputum production, sore throat, breathlessness, chest pain, palpitations, fever, fatigue, headache, dizziness, sleep disturbances (insomnia or excessive somnolence), loss of concentration, memory disturbances, peripheral tingling or numbness, nausea and vomiting, diarrhea, abdominal pain, reduced appetite, joint pain, muscle pain, depression, anxiety, loss of smell, loss of taste, skin rash, and hair loss. The presence of symptoms at the follow-up interview was counted as persistent long COVID. Participants with long COVID symptoms were asked to provide a binary response indicating whether their symptoms impacted their daily living. This impact was recorded as either present or absent.

HRQoL was assessed and presented as a five-level impact on each health domain of the EQ-5D-5L questionnaire consisting of mobility, self-care, usual activities, pain/discomfort, and anxiety/depression. The utility value was calculated from the EQ-5D-5L results based on Thai algorithm31. The utility values range from 0 to 1 which represent health status equivalent to death and perfect health, respectively.

sec.title: Statistical analysis
sec.p: Categorical and continuous variables were presented as frequency with percentage and mean with standard deviation, respectively. The association between each symptom and its impact on daily living, in terms of presence or absence, was determined using Fisher’s exact test. Differences between continuous variables were identified using either Student’s t-test or the Mann-Whitney U test. The selection of the appropriate test was based on the results of the Shapiro-Wilk test for normality of distribution. The Benjamini–Hochberg method was used to adjust p-values for multiple testing in both the analyses of symptom impact on daily living and the comparisons across EQ-5D-5L domains. To compare the median difference in utility scores between participants with and without long COVID symptoms with the previously reported minimally important difference, we used the Hodges–Lehmann estimator to quantify the median difference between groups.

The prevalence of long COVID at the three-month follow-up was reported as a crude estimate. Conditional prevalence was then assessed at one year and two years post-infection: long COVID at one year among participants who reported symptoms at the three-month follow-up, and long COVID at two years among participants who reported symptoms at the one-year follow-up. These conditional prevalences were presented both as crude estimates and as IPW weighted estimates to account for loss to follow-up.

To identify predictive factors of long COVID and long COVID with reported impact on daily living, multiple logistic regression was performed using the iteratively weighted least squares (IWLS) method. The estimated aOR and 95% confidence intervals were reported. Goodness of fit for the logistic regression model was assessed using deviance goodness-of-fit test and Hosmer–Lemeshow test. All analyses were considered statistically significant when the p-value was less than 0.05. All statistical analyses were conducted using the R Statistical Package, version 4.3.2.

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: This study examined long COVID among hospitalized patients in Thailand over a two-year follow-up. We found the prevalence of long COVID of 49.8% at three months post-infection. The conditional prevalence in participants who reported long COVID in the previous visit of 64.4% and 22% at the one-year and two-year follow-ups, respectively. Severity of acute COVID-19, vaccination status and sex were identified as predictors for developing long COVID that impact daily living assessed at three months post-infection. Lower HRQoL among participant with long COVID compared with those without long COVID was observed across every follow-ups. Our findings underline the continuing impact of long COVID symptoms on quality of life. Further research in diverse and larger populations is needed to confirm the findings in this study.

sec.title: Supplementary Information
sec.p: Below is the link to the electronic supplementary material.

fn.p: Springer Nature remains neutral with regard to jurisdictional claims in published maps and institutional affiliations.

ack.title: Acknowledgements
ack.p: The authors would like to express their gratitude to the Medical Records Division of Siriraj Hospital for their invaluable assistance in retrieving the patient data essential for this study. We also wish to thank Euarat Mepramoon for kindly providing assistance with manuscript preparation.

notes.title: Author contributions
notes.p: PL: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Project administration, Resources, Validation, Visualization, Writing—original draft, Writing—review & editingAT: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Visualization, Writing—review & editingWW: Data curation, Investigation, Project administration, Writing—review & editingCK: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Methodology, Validation, Visualization, Writing—review & editingPP: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Methodology, Validation, Visualization, Writing—review & editing.

notes.title: Data availability
notes.p: The data that support the findings of this study are available on request from the corresponding author, (PL). The data are not publicly available due to restrictions e.g. their containing information that could compromise the privacy of research participants.

notes.title: Competing interests
notes.p: The authors declare no competing interests.

notes.title: Informed consent statement
notes.p: All data collection processes were carried out following ethical approval, with informed consent obtained verbally from participants.

notes.title: Conflict of interest statement
notes.p: The authors have no conflicts of interest to declare.

notes.title: Institutional review board statement
notes.p: The study was reviewed and approved by the institutional review board at the Faculty of Medicine Siriraj Hospital, Mahidol University (Certificate of Approval number: Si 961/2021).

notes.title: Supporting information
notes.p: Two supplementary figures and 4 supplementary tables.

element-citation.article-title: Global prevalence of Post-Coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) condition or long COVID: A Meta-Analysis and systematic review
